question:
A-4. Given a sequence \( \left\{x_{n}\right\}, n=1,2, \cdots \), such that \( \operatorname{limit}_{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{x_{n}-x_{n-2}\right\}=0 \). Prove that
\[
\operatorname{limit}_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{x_{n}-x_{n-1}}{n}=0
\]
solution:
A-4 For \( \epsilon>0 \), let \( N \) be sufficiently large so that \( \left|x_{n}-x_{n-2}\right|<\epsilon \) for all \( n \geqq N \). Note that for any \( n>N \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{n}-x_{n-1}= & \left(x_{n}-x_{n-2}\right)-\left(x_{n-1}-x_{n-3}\right)+\left(x_{n-2}-x_{n-8}\right)-\cdots \\
& \pm\left(x_{N+1}-x_{N-1}\right) \mp\left(x_{N}-x_{N-1}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( \left|x_{n}-x_{n-1}\right| \leqq(n-N) \epsilon+\left|x_{N}-x_{N-1}\right| \) and \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(x_{n}-x_{n-1}\right) / n=0 \).

descriptive_long.question:
A-4. Given a sequence \( \left\{seqelem\right\}, indexvar=1,2, \cdots \), such that \( \operatorname{limit}_{indexvar \rightarrow \infty}\left\{seqelem-seqminustwo\right\}=0 \). Prove that
\[
\operatorname{limit}_{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \frac{seqelem-seqminusone}{indexvar}=0
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-4 For \( tolerance>0 \), let \( boundvar \) be sufficiently large so that \( \left|seqelem-seqminustwo\right|<tolerance \) for all \( indexvar \geqq boundvar \). Note that for any \( indexvar>boundvar \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
seqelem-seqminusone= & \left(seqelem-seqminustwo\right)-\left(seqminusone-seqminusthree\right)+\left(seqminustwo-seqminuseight\right)-\cdots \\
& \pm\left(seqplusone-seqnminusone\right) \mp\left(seqbound-seqnminusone\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( \left|seqelem-seqminusone\right| \leqq(indexvar-boundvar)\, tolerance+\left|seqbound-seqnminusone\right| \) and \( \lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty}\left(seqelem-seqminusone\right) / indexvar=0 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-4. Given a sequence \( \left\{bluewhale\right\}, sandcastle=1,2, \cdots \), such that \( \operatorname{limit}_{sandcastle \rightarrow \infty}\left\{bluewhale-redtulip\right\}=0 \). Prove that
\[
\operatorname{limit}_{sandcastle \rightarrow \infty} \frac{bluewhale-greentea}{sandcastle}=0
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-4 For \( rainbowfish>0 \), let \( stonebridge \) be sufficiently large so that \( \left|bluewhale-redtulip\right|<rainbowfish \) for all \( sandcastle \geqq stonebridge \). Note that for any \( sandcastle>stonebridge \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
bluewhale-greentea= & \left(bluewhale-redtulip\right)-\left(greentea-yellowmaple\right)+\left(redtulip-silvermoon\right)-\cdots \\
& \pm\left(orangepeel-purpleiris\right) \mp\left(whitecloud-purpleiris\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( \left|bluewhale-greentea\right| \leqq(sandcastle-stonebridge) rainbowfish+\left|whitecloud-purpleiris\right| \) and \( \lim _{sandcastle \rightarrow \infty}\left(bluewhale-greentea\right) / sandcastle=0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
A-4. Given a sequence \( \left\{constantseq\right\}, constantindx=1,2, \cdots \), such that \( \operatorname{limit}_{constantindx \rightarrow \infty}\left\{constantseq-fixedtwoago\right\}=0 \). Prove that
\[
\operatorname{limit}_{constantindx \rightarrow \infty} \frac{constantseq-fixedoneago}{constantindx}=0
\]
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
A-4 For \( largedeviate>0 \), let \( variablelimit \) be sufficiently large so that \( \left|constantseq-fixedtwoago\right|<largedeviate \) for all \( constantindx \geqq variablelimit \). Note that for any \( constantindx>variablelimit \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
constantseq-fixedoneago= & \left(constantseq-fixedtwoago\right)-\left(fixedoneago-fixedthreeago\right)+\left(fixedtwoago-fixedeightago\right)-\cdots \\
& \pm\left(fixedafter-fixedbefore\right) \mp\left(fixedcurrent-fixedbefore\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( \left|constantseq-fixedoneago\right| \leqq(constantindx-variablelimit) largedeviate+\left|fixedcurrent-fixedbefore\right| \) and \( \lim _{constantindx \rightarrow \infty}\left(constantseq-fixedoneago\right) / constantindx=0 \).
>>>

garbled_string.question:
A-4. Given a sequence \( \left\{qzxwvtnp\right\}, wprhslqa=1,2, \cdots \), such that \( \operatorname{limit}_{wprhslqa \rightarrow \infty}\left\{qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla\right\}=0 \). Prove that
\[
\operatorname{limit}_{wprhslqa \rightarrow \infty} \frac{qzxwvtnp-mpltrnck}{wprhslqa}=0
\]
garbled_string.solution:
A-4 For \( zpeiyxwr>0 \), let \( rtxvbksm \) be sufficiently large so that \( \left|qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla\right|<zpeiyxwr \) for all \( wprhslqa \geqq rtxvbksm \). Note that for any \( wprhslqa>rtxvbksm \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp-mpltrnck= & \left(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla\right)-\left(mpltrnck-vbndkqwe\right)+\left(hjgrksla-fslpdmrz\right)-\cdots \\
& \pm\left(lkjnhgry-wqptrsdv\right) \mp\left(cgdmprsl-wqptrsdv\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( \left|qzxwvtnp-mpltrnck\right| \leqq(wprhslqa-rtxvbksm) zpeiyxwr+\left|cgdmprsl-wqptrsdv\right| \) and \( \lim _{wprhslqa \rightarrow \infty}\left(qzxwvtnp-mpltrnck\right) / wprhslqa=0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $1<m_{1}<m_{2}<\dots <m_{s}$ be fixed positive integers with $s\ge 2$ and let $(X,\lVert\cdot\rVert)$ be an arbitrary real Banach space.  
For a sequence $(x_{n})_{n\ge 0}\subset X$ assume that, for every $j=1,\dots ,s$, the limit  
\[
L_{j}\;:=\;\lim_{n\to\infty}\bigl(x_{n}-x_{\,n-m_{j}}\bigr)
\tag{$\ast$}
\]
exists (in $X$).

(a) Prove that the following two statements are equivalent.

(a$_1$) The normalised sequence $x_{n}/n$ converges in $X$; that is, the limit  
\[
\lambda\;:=\;\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n}}{n}
\]
exists in $X$.

(a$_2$) All quotients $L_{j}/m_{j}$ coincide; equivalently, there is a vector $\lambda\in X$ such that  
\[
L_{j}\;=\;\lambda\,m_{j}\qquad\text{for every }j=1,\dots ,s .
\]

(b) \emph{Assuming that the equivalent assertions in (a) hold}, show that for every integer $r\ge 1$
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}-\lambda r}{n}=0 ,
\tag{$\clubsuit$}
\]
that is, $x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}= \lambda r + o(n)$.

(c) \emph{Again under the hypotheses of (a) (equivalently of (b))}, prove that if $L_{j}=0$ for at least one index $j$ then necessarily $\lambda =0$ and, consequently,
\[
x_{n}=o(n)\quad\text{and}\quad x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}=o(n)\quad\text{for every fixed }r\ge 1 .
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put \(\Delta_{k}x_{n}:=x_{n}-x_{\,n-k}\) (defined for \(n\ge k\)).

\textbf{Step 1.  Implication \((a_{1}\Rightarrow a_{2})\).}  
Assume \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n}}{n}= \lambda\) and define the remainder sequence
\[
a_{n}:=x_{n}-\lambda n\qquad(n\ge 0).
\]
Then \(a_{n}/n\to 0\).

Fix \(j\in\{1,\dots ,s\}\).  For \(n\ge m_{j}\),
\[
\Delta_{m_{j}}x_{n}= \lambda m_{j} + (a_{n}-a_{\,n-m_{j}}).
\tag{1}
\]
Because the limit \((\ast)\) exists, the difference sequence has a limit
\[
\delta_{j}:=\lim_{n\to\infty}(a_{n}-a_{\,n-m_{j}})\in X .
\tag{2}
\]

\emph{Claim.} \(\delta_{j}=0\).

Suppose to the contrary that \(\delta_{j}\ne 0\).  
By the Hahn-Banach theorem there is \(f\in X^{\ast}\) with \(\lVert f\rVert=1\) and \(f(\delta_{j})=\lVert\delta_{j}\rVert>0\).
Applying \(f\) to (2) and arguing as in the original text yields, for a suitable \(\varepsilon>0\),
\[
\lVert a_{N+r+qm_{j}}\rVert \ge q\varepsilon - C\qquad(q\ge 0),
\]
whence \(\lVert a_{n}\rVert/n\not\to 0\), contradicting \(a_{n}/n\to 0\).  
Thus \(\delta_{j}=0\), and (1) now implies
\[
L_{j}= \lambda m_{j}.
\]
Hence all quotients \(L_{j}/m_{j}\) equal \(\lambda\); therefore \((a_{2})\) holds.

\textbf{Step 2.  Implication \((a_{2}\Rightarrow a_{1})\).}  
Assume \(L_{j}= \lambda m_{j}\;(j=1,\dots ,s)\) and keep the definition of \(a_{n}\).  
Then \((\ast)\) reads \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\Delta_{m_{j}}a_{n}=0\) for every \(j\), in particular for \(j=1\).

Fix \(\varepsilon>0\).  Choose \(N\) so that \(\lVert\Delta_{m_{1}}a_{p}\rVert<\varepsilon\) for all \(p\ge N\).  
For \(n\ge N\) write \(n=qm_{1}+r\) with \(0\le r<m_{1}\).  A telescoping sum gives
\[
a_{n}= \sum_{k=0}^{q-1}\bigl(a_{\,r+(k+1)m_{1}}-a_{\,r+km_{1}}\bigr)+a_{r}.
\]
Each summand with index at least \(N\) has norm \(\le\varepsilon\), and there are at most \(q\) such terms; the remaining finitely many terms are uniformly bounded.  Hence
\[
\lVert a_{n}\rVert\le q\varepsilon + C,
\]
so that \(\lVert a_{n}\rVert/n\le \varepsilon/m_{1}+C/n\to\varepsilon/m_{1}\).  
Letting \(\varepsilon\to 0\) yields \(a_{n}/n\to 0\), i.e.\ \(x_{n}/n\to\lambda\).  
Thus \((a_{1})\) holds.

Consequently \((a_{1})\) and \((a_{2})\) are equivalent.

\textbf{Step 3.  Proof of \((\clubsuit)\).}  
With \(a_{n}\) as above,
\[
x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}-\lambda r = a_{n}-a_{\,n-r}.
\]
Dividing by \(n\) and using \(a_{k}/k\to 0\) we obtain
\[
\frac{x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}-\lambda r}{n} = \frac{a_{n}}{n}-\frac{a_{\,n-r}}{n}\;\longrightarrow\;0,
\]
proving \((\clubsuit)\).

\textbf{Step 4.  Part (c).}  
Under the hypotheses of (a) (hence of (b)), assume \(L_{j_{0}}=0\) for some \(j_{0}\).  
From (a$_2$) we have \(L_{j}= \lambda m_{j}\;(j=1,\dots ,s)\); thus
\[
\lambda=\frac{L_{j_{0}}}{m_{\,j_{0}}}=0 .
\]
With \(\lambda=0\) the definition of \(a_{n}\) gives \(a_{n}=x_{n}\).  
Step 2 then yields \(\lVert x_{n}\rVert/n\to 0\), i.e.\ \(x_{n}=o(n)\).  
Substituting \(\lambda=0\) into \((\clubsuit)\) further gives
\[
\frac{x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}}{n}\to 0 \quad(r\ge 1),
\]
hence \(x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}=o(n)\) for every fixed \(r\ge 1\).  \(\qed\)

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Multiple interacting constraints: limits of s distinct step–differences must be handled simultaneously, not just one.  
2. Logical equivalence: the problem asks for an “if and only if’’ statement linking those limits to the existence of lim xₙ/n, increasing conceptual depth.  
3. Banach-space setting: arguments must avoid scalar ordering and use norm estimates, demanding knowledge of functional analysis.  
4. General asymptotic formula (♣) for every r requires a refined telescoping argument combined with the limit already proved, far beyond the single-step estimate in the original.  
5. The gcd-1 hypothesis introduces number-theoretic structure (it guarantees that every r can be decomposed in terms of m₁, … , m_s), adding an extra theoretical layer.

Overall the enhanced variant requires deeper insight into difference equations in Banach spaces, simultaneous limit constraints, and a careful combination of analytic and number-theoretic techniques—substantially harder than both the original problem and the previous kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $1<m_{1}<m_{2}<\dots <m_{s}$ be fixed positive integers with $s\ge 2$ and let $(X,\lVert\cdot\rVert)$ be an arbitrary real Banach space.  
For a sequence $(x_{n})_{n\ge 0}\subset X$ assume that, for every $j=1,\dots ,s$, the limit  
\[
L_{j}\;:=\;\lim_{n\to\infty}\bigl(x_{n}-x_{\,n-m_{j}}\bigr)
\tag{$\ast$}
\]
exists (in $X$).

(a) Prove that the following two statements are equivalent.

(a$_1$) The normalised sequence $x_{n}/n$ converges in $X$; that is, the limit  
\[
\lambda\;:=\;\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n}}{n}
\]
exists in $X$.

(a$_2$) All quotients $L_{j}/m_{j}$ coincide; equivalently, there is a vector $\lambda\in X$ such that  
\[
L_{j}\;=\;\lambda\,m_{j}\qquad\text{for every }j=1,\dots ,s .
\]

(b) \emph{Assuming that the equivalent assertions in (a) hold}, show that for every integer $r\ge 1$
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}-\lambda r}{n}=0 ,
\tag{$\clubsuit$}
\]
that is, $x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}= \lambda r + o(n)$.

(c) \emph{Again under the hypotheses of (a) (equivalently of (b))}, prove that if $L_{j}=0$ for at least one index $j$ then necessarily $\lambda =0$ and, consequently,
\[
x_{n}=o(n)\quad\text{and}\quad x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}=o(n)\quad\text{for every fixed }r\ge 1 .
\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put \(\Delta_{k}x_{n}:=x_{n}-x_{\,n-k}\) (defined for \(n\ge k\)).

\textbf{Step 1.  Implication \((a_{1}\Rightarrow a_{2})\).}  
Assume \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n}}{n}= \lambda\) and define the remainder sequence
\[
a_{n}:=x_{n}-\lambda n\qquad(n\ge 0).
\]
Then \(a_{n}/n\to 0\).

Fix \(j\in\{1,\dots ,s\}\).  For \(n\ge m_{j}\),
\[
\Delta_{m_{j}}x_{n}= \lambda m_{j} + (a_{n}-a_{\,n-m_{j}}).
\tag{1}
\]
Because the limit \((\ast)\) exists, the difference sequence has a limit
\[
\delta_{j}:=\lim_{n\to\infty}(a_{n}-a_{\,n-m_{j}})\in X .
\tag{2}
\]

\emph{Claim.} \(\delta_{j}=0\).

Suppose to the contrary that \(\delta_{j}\ne 0\).  
By the Hahn-Banach theorem there is \(f\in X^{\ast}\) with \(\lVert f\rVert=1\) and \(f(\delta_{j})=\lVert\delta_{j}\rVert>0\).
Applying \(f\) to (2) and arguing as in the original text yields, for a suitable \(\varepsilon>0\),
\[
\lVert a_{N+r+qm_{j}}\rVert \ge q\varepsilon - C\qquad(q\ge 0),
\]
whence \(\lVert a_{n}\rVert/n\not\to 0\), contradicting \(a_{n}/n\to 0\).  
Thus \(\delta_{j}=0\), and (1) now implies
\[
L_{j}= \lambda m_{j}.
\]
Hence all quotients \(L_{j}/m_{j}\) equal \(\lambda\); therefore \((a_{2})\) holds.

\textbf{Step 2.  Implication \((a_{2}\Rightarrow a_{1})\).}  
Assume \(L_{j}= \lambda m_{j}\;(j=1,\dots ,s)\) and keep the definition of \(a_{n}\).  
Then \((\ast)\) reads \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\Delta_{m_{j}}a_{n}=0\) for every \(j\), in particular for \(j=1\).

Fix \(\varepsilon>0\).  Choose \(N\) so that \(\lVert\Delta_{m_{1}}a_{p}\rVert<\varepsilon\) for all \(p\ge N\).  
For \(n\ge N\) write \(n=qm_{1}+r\) with \(0\le r<m_{1}\).  A telescoping sum gives
\[
a_{n}= \sum_{k=0}^{q-1}\bigl(a_{\,r+(k+1)m_{1}}-a_{\,r+km_{1}}\bigr)+a_{r}.
\]
Each summand with index at least \(N\) has norm \(\le\varepsilon\), and there are at most \(q\) such terms; the remaining finitely many terms are uniformly bounded.  Hence
\[
\lVert a_{n}\rVert\le q\varepsilon + C,
\]
so that \(\lVert a_{n}\rVert/n\le \varepsilon/m_{1}+C/n\to\varepsilon/m_{1}\).  
Letting \(\varepsilon\to 0\) yields \(a_{n}/n\to 0\), i.e.\ \(x_{n}/n\to\lambda\).  
Thus \((a_{1})\) holds.

Consequently \((a_{1})\) and \((a_{2})\) are equivalent.

\textbf{Step 3.  Proof of \((\clubsuit)\).}  
With \(a_{n}\) as above,
\[
x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}-\lambda r = a_{n}-a_{\,n-r}.
\]
Dividing by \(n\) and using \(a_{k}/k\to 0\) we obtain
\[
\frac{x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}-\lambda r}{n} = \frac{a_{n}}{n}-\frac{a_{\,n-r}}{n}\;\longrightarrow\;0,
\]
proving \((\clubsuit)\).

\textbf{Step 4.  Part (c).}  
Under the hypotheses of (a) (hence of (b)), assume \(L_{j_{0}}=0\) for some \(j_{0}\).  
From (a$_2$) we have \(L_{j}= \lambda m_{j}\;(j=1,\dots ,s)\); thus
\[
\lambda=\frac{L_{j_{0}}}{m_{\,j_{0}}}=0 .
\]
With \(\lambda=0\) the definition of \(a_{n}\) gives \(a_{n}=x_{n}\).  
Step 2 then yields \(\lVert x_{n}\rVert/n\to 0\), i.e.\ \(x_{n}=o(n)\).  
Substituting \(\lambda=0\) into \((\clubsuit)\) further gives
\[
\frac{x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}}{n}\to 0 \quad(r\ge 1),
\]
hence \(x_{n}-x_{\,n-r}=o(n)\) for every fixed \(r\ge 1\).  \(\qed\)

Overall the enhanced variant requires deeper insight into difference equations in Banach spaces, simultaneous limit constraints, and a careful combination of analytic and number-theoretic techniques—substantially harder than both the original problem and the previous kernel variant.
